title: Normative reconstructions of property rights in German classical philosophy [Review Essay]
author: Ovcharuk

opening_move.description: The review essay opens by arguing that German idealism (Kant, Fichte, Hegel) has been unjustly neglected in political theory of property, which remains dominated by Lockean rights-based and Humean utilitarian frameworks. Two recent systematic reconstructions reinstate these philosophers as critics of property's perennial political problem.
opening_move.stakes_claimed: A German idealist reconstruction of property theory offers normative resources beyond liberal rights-based and utilitarian accounts — recognition and pluralism as alternative foundations

theoretical_positioning.description: Review essay presenting and critically assessing two books (Blumenfeld; James) that reconstruct Kant's, Fichte's, and Hegel's theories of property as normative alternatives to dominant liberal frameworks. The reviewer endorses the reconstructive project while noting limits.

epistemic_mode.interpretive_methods: intellectual history; close reading of German idealist texts; comparative reconstruction of property theories
epistemic_mode.notes: Review essay (6 pages). Primarily expository and critical assessment of two philosophical books. The philosophical texts (Kant, Fichte, Hegel as reconstructed by Blumenfeld and James) are the interpretive cases through which the normative argument about property theory is made.

case_type.case_name: Blumenfeld, The Concept of Property in Kant, Fichte, and Hegel (2023); James, Property and its Forms in Classical German Philosophy (2023)

confidence_notes: Review essay (6 pages). Classification as normative_with_interpretive_case reflects the use of philosophical texts as interpretive objects in service of a normative argument about what property theory should look like.
